content:
# Exodus Chapter 3 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32v–33r (PDF pp. 63–64); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 43–44 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_003_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_003_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_003_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_003_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** And Mosheh was shepherding the flock of Yitro his father-in-law, priest of Midyan; and he led the flock beyond the wilderness, and he came to the mountain of God, to Horev.

**2** And the mal'akh [messenger/angel] of YHWH appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of the seneh [thornbush]; and he looked, and behold, the seneh [was] burning in the fire, and the seneh was not consumed.

**3** And Mosheh said: "Let me turn aside now and see this great sight -- why the seneh does not burn-up."

**4** And YHWH saw that he turned aside to see; and God called to him from the midst of the seneh, and he said: "Mosheh, Mosheh." And he said: "Here-I-[am]."

**5** And he said: "Do not draw near to here; remove your sandals from upon your feet, for the place which you [are] standing upon it [is] ground of holiness."

**6** And he said: "I [am] the God of your father, the God of Avraham, the God of Yitshaq, and the God of Ya'aqov." And Mosheh hid his face, for he was afraid from looking toward God.

**7** And YHWH said: "Seeing I have seen the affliction of my people who [are] in Mitsrayim, and their cry I have heard from the face of their taskmasters, for I know their pains.

**8** And I have come down to deliver them from the hand of Mitsrayim and to bring them up from that land to a land good and wide, to a land flowing [with] milk and honey, to the place of the Kena'ani and the Hitti and the Emori and the Perizzi and the Hivvi and the Yevusi.

**9** And now, behold, the cry of the sons of Yisra'el has come to me, and also I have seen the oppression with which Mitsrayim [is] oppressing them.

**10** And now, go, and I will send you to Par'oh; and bring-out my people, the sons of Yisra'el, from Mitsrayim."

**11** And Mosheh said to God: "Who [am] I that I should go to Par'oh, and that I should bring-out the sons of Yisra'el from Mitsrayim?"

**12** And he said: "For Ehyeh [I-will-be] with you; and this [is] to you the sign that I have sent you: when you bring-out the people from Mitsrayim, you-shall-serve God upon this mountain."

**13** And Mosheh said to God: "Behold, I come to the sons of Yisra'el and I say to them: 'The God of your fathers has sent me to you,' and they say to me: 'What [is] his name?' -- what shall I say to them?"

**14** And God said to Mosheh: "Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh [I-Am Who I-Am / I-Will-Be Who I-Will-Be]." And he said: "Thus you shall say to the sons of Yisra'el: 'Ehyeh [I-Am / I-Will-Be] has sent me to you.'"

**15** And God said again to Mosheh: "Thus you shall say to the sons of Yisra'el: 'YHWH, the God of your fathers, the God of Avraham, the God of Yitshaq, and the God of Ya'aqov, has sent me to you.' This [is] my name le-'olam [for ever], and this [is] my memorial to generation [and] generation.

**16** Go and gather the elders of Yisra'el and say to them: 'YHWH, the God of your fathers, appeared to me, the God of Avraham, Yitshaq, and Ya'aqov, saying: Visiting I have visited you and [I have seen] what is being done to you in Mitsrayim.

**17** And I said: I will bring you up from the affliction of Mitsrayim to the land of the Kena'ani and the Hitti and the Emori and the Perizzi and the Hivvi and the Yevusi, to a land flowing [with] milk and honey.'

**18** And they will listen to your voice; and you shall come, you and the elders of Yisra'el, to the king of Mitsrayim, and you-shall-say to him: 'YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, has encountered us; and now, let us go, please, a journey of three days into the wilderness, and let us sacrifice to YHWH our God.'

**19** And I know that the king of Mitsrayim will not give you [permission] to go, and not by a strong hand.

**20** And I will stretch out my hand and I will strike Mitsrayim with all my wonders which I will do in its midst; and after that he will send you out.

**21** And I will give the favor of this people in the eyes of Mitsrayim; and it shall be that when you go, you shall not go empty.

**22** And [each] woman shall ask of her neighbor and of the one sojourning in her house, vessels of silver and vessels of gold and garments; and you shall put [them] upon your sons and upon your daughters; and you shall despoil Mitsrayim."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A)

1. **v.1 -- Yitro:** The father-in-law of Mosheh is called Yitro (yitro) here, whereas in 2:18 he was called Re'u'el. The Leningrad Codex reads yitro khotno clearly at the start of the chapter. The name Yitro is traditionally understood as meaning "his excellence" or "his abundance" (from y-t-r, "to remain over, exceed"). The LXX (Vaticanus p. 43) reads Iothor, a direct transliteration. The multiple names for Mosheh's father-in-law (Re'u'el, Yitro, Hovav) remain one of the classic puzzles of the Pentateuchal text.

2. **v.1 -- Horev (chorev):** The Leningrad Codex reads chorevah with the locative he suffix, indicating "to/toward Horev." Horev is the name used for the mountain of divine revelation in certain Pentateuchal traditions, while Sinai is used in others. The root ch-r-v means "dry, desolate." The LXX reads Choreb, transliterating directly. The phrase "mountain of God" (har ha-elohim) may be a proleptic description -- it is called the mountain of God because of what is about to occur there.

3. **v.2 -- mal'akh YHWH:** The Leningrad Codex reads mal'akh yhwh clearly. The mal'akh (messenger/angel) of YHWH appears in the flame, yet by v. 4 it is YHWH and Elohim who speak directly. This fluidity between the mal'akh and YHWH himself is characteristic of the theophanic angel passages (cf. Genesis 16:7-13; 22:11-18; Judges 6:11-24). The LXX reads aggelos kuriou ("angel/messenger of the Lord"), using kurios for the divine name as is its consistent practice.

4. **v.2 -- seneh:** The thornbush (seneh) is a distinctive word appearing only here and in Deuteronomy 33:16 in the entire Hebrew Bible. Many scholars note the phonetic play between sinai and seneh. The Leningrad Codex writes ha-seneh with the definite article throughout. The LXX translates as batos ("bramble/thornbush"). The text specifies the seneh was "burning in the fire" (bo'er ba-esh) yet "not consumed" (eynenu ukkal) -- the niphal participle ukkal from '-k-l ("to eat/consume") indicates the bush was not being devoured/eaten by the fire.

5. **v.4 -- "Mosheh, Mosheh":** The double calling of the name is a significant literary pattern in the Hebrew Bible, marking moments of critical divine address (cf. "Avraham, Avraham" in Genesis 22:11; "Shemu'el, Shemu'el" in 1 Samuel 3:10). The Leningrad Codex text here reads mosheh mosheh with no intervening pause mark. The LXX (Vaticanus) also doubles the name: Mouses Mouses. The Masoretic note in some manuscripts places a pesiq (vertical bar) between the two occurrences, but the Leningrad Codex does not clearly show one at this point.

6. **v.5 -- "ground of holiness" (admat qodesh):** Literally "ground of holiness" -- a construct chain. I preserve the Hebrew construction rather than rendering "holy ground." The holiness belongs to the ground as a quality of the divine presence upon it. The LXX renders ge hagia ("holy ground"), using the adjective rather than preserving the genitive construction.

7. **v.6 -- "the God of your father" (elohey avikha):** The Leningrad Codex reads the singular "your father" (avikha) in the first clause, followed by the threefold enumeration of Avraham, Yitshaq, and Ya'aqov. The LXX reads ho theos tou patros sou ("the God of your father"), matching the MT singular. The Samaritan Pentateuch and some LXX manuscripts read the plural "your fathers" (avotekha) here, harmonizing with the plural in v. 13, 15, 16. The EMET follows the Leningrad Codex singular.

8. **v.7 -- "Seeing I have seen" (ra'oh ra'iti):** The Hebrew infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (ra'oh ra'iti) intensifies the action: "I have certainly/surely seen." This emphatic construction is characteristic of biblical Hebrew and cannot be reproduced by a single English verb. I render it woodenly as "Seeing I have seen" to preserve the doubling. The LXX renders idon eidon ("seeing I have seen"), replicating the Hebrew construction in Greek.

9. **v.8 -- The six peoples:** The Leningrad Codex lists six peoples: Kena'ani, Hitti, Emori, Perizzi, Hivvi, Yevusi. Other passages list seven (adding the Girgashi). The LXX (Vaticanus) follows the same six-people list. The order varies across different biblical passages, but the Leningrad Codex order here is consistent.

10. **v.8 -- "flowing milk and honey" (zavat halav u-devash):** A feminine singular participle zavat ("flowing") modifying erets ("land"). The image is of a land so abundantly fertile that milk and honey flow from it. Devash may refer to bee-honey or, as some scholars argue, to date-syrup. The phrase recurs throughout the Pentateuch as a fixed description of the promised land.

11. **v.12 -- Ehyeh (ehyeh):** The first occurrence of this critical word. YHWH says ki ehyeh immakh, "for I-will-be with you." The word ehyeh is the first person singular qal imperfect of the root h-y-h ("to be"). It can mean "I am" or "I will be" -- the Hebrew imperfect encompasses both present and future. This same word becomes the divine name revelation in v. 14. I transliterate it and provide the literal rendering in brackets. The LXX renders hoti esomai meta sou ("for I will be with you"), using the future tense.

12. **v.14 -- Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh:** This is the supreme divine self-disclosure in the Hebrew Bible. The Leningrad Codex reads ehyeh asher ehyeh clearly. The phrase consists of: ehyeh ("I am / I will be") + asher ("who / which / that") + ehyeh ("I am / I will be"). The range of possible renderings includes: "I Am Who I Am," "I Will Be Who I Will Be," "I Will Be What I Will Be," "I Am What I Am," and "I Cause To Be What I Cause To Be" (if taken as hiphil causative). The Hebrew imperfect verb resists reduction to a single English tense. The LXX renders ego eimi ho on ("I am the Being / I am the One Who Is"), which shifts the meaning toward ontological existence in the Greek philosophical sense -- a significant interpretive move away from the dynamic, promissory force of the Hebrew ehyeh. The EMET preserves the Hebrew transliteration with a dual bracketed rendering to honor the ambiguity.

13. **v.14 -- "Ehyeh has sent me":** In the second half of the verse, God instructs Mosheh to tell the sons of Yisra'el: "Ehyeh sent me to you." Here Ehyeh functions as a proper name -- a first-person divine self-designation. The LXX renders ho on apestalken me ("the Being/the One Who Is has sent me"). This is remarkable: the divine name, when communicated to others, shifts from first person (ehyeh, "I am/will be") to third person (YHWH, "He is/will be") in v. 15.

14. **v.15 -- YHWH as "my name le-'olam":** The Leningrad Codex reads zeh shemi le-'olam ve-zeh zikhri le-dor dor ("this is my name for-ever, and this is my memorial to generation [and] generation"). The word le-'olam is written in the Leningrad Codex as l-'-l-m. There is a well-known Masoretic tradition that the ketiv (written form) here is le-'alem ("to conceal"), while the qere (reading tradition) is le-'olam ("forever"). This has generated the rabbinic teaching that the divine name is to be "concealed" -- not pronounced as written. The LXX reads eis ton aiona ("unto the age"), following the qere/standard reading.

15. **v.15 -- zikkaron (zikhri, "my memorial/remembrance"):** The word zekher/zikkaron denotes more than passive memory -- it is an active invocation. "My memorial" (zikhri) means the name by which God is to be actively remembered, invoked, and called upon. The LXX uses mnemosunon ("memorial").

16. **v.16 -- "Visiting I have visited" (paqod paqadti):** Another infinitive absolute construction, parallel to "seeing I have seen" in v. 7. The root p-q-d means "to visit, attend to, take account of, muster." When God is the subject, it often implies both attention and intervention. The LXX renders episkope epeskemai ("visiting I have visited"), again replicating the Hebrew emphatic form.

17. **v.18 -- "YHWH the God of the Hebrews has encountered us" (niqrah 'aleinu):** The niphal of q-r-h, "has encountered/met us." The diplomatic phrasing presented to Par'oh -- that YHWH "encountered" them -- is less confrontational than "commanded" or "sent." The LXX reads proskeketai hemas ("has summoned us"), a slightly different nuance.

18. **v.19 -- "and not by a strong hand" (ve-lo' be-yad hazaqah):** This clause is difficult. It can be read as: (a) "not even by a strong hand" (meaning Par'oh will not let them go even when compelled), or (b) "except by a strong hand" (lo' functioning as an emphatic asseveration). The LXX reads ean me meta cheiros krataias ("unless/except with a strong hand"), taking the clause as an exception -- Par'oh will not release them unless a strong hand compels him. Most interpretive traditions follow the LXX reading. The EMET preserves the ambiguous MT phrasing.

19. **v.21 -- "I will give the favor of this people" (ve-natatti et hen ha-'am ha-zeh):** YHWH grants favor (hen) -- the favorable disposition of the Egyptians toward the Israelites. The noun hen ("grace, favor") is given as a divine gift, not earned. The LXX reads doso de charin to lao touto ("I will give grace to this people"), closely matching the Hebrew.

20. **v.22 -- "you shall despoil" (ve-nitsaltem):** The verb n-ts-l in the piel means "to strip, plunder, despoil." The Leningrad Codex reads ve-nitsaltem et Mitsrayim. The idea is that the Israelites will "strip" or "plunder" the Egyptians of their wealth -- a reversal of the plundering that slavery itself constituted. The LXX reads kai skuleusite tous Aiguptious ("and you shall despoil the Egyptians"), using the verb skuleuo (to strip armor/spoil from the fallen). This is not theft but restitution -- wages for centuries of unpaid labor. The same verb n-ts-l in a different stem (hiphil) means "to deliver/rescue," creating a wordplay: God delivers (matsil) his people, and his people despoil (menatsalim) their oppressors.

21. **v.22 -- "shall ask" (ve-sha'alah):** The root sh-'-l means "to ask, request, borrow." It is debated whether this implies borrowing (with intent to return) or simply asking (with no return expected). The context of despoiling (nitsaltem) at the end of the verse makes clear these goods are not being borrowed. The LXX reads aitesei ("shall ask/request"), which carries no implication of returning. The same root sh-'-l gives us the name Sha'ul (Saul, "asked [of God]").

22. **v.22 -- "vessels of silver and vessels of gold and garments" (keley khesef u-khley zahav u-semalot):** The word keli (plural keley) means "vessel, implement, article" -- a general term for crafted goods. Semalot ("garments") are outer clothing. The three categories -- silver articles, gold articles, clothing -- represent comprehensive wealth transfer.

### Vaticanus LXX Comparison Summary

The Vaticanus manuscript (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 43-44) preserves Exodus 3 in continuous Greek uncial across three columns per page. The chapter begins on page 43 (marked CAP. XLVIII in the Vaticanus numbering system, continuing from the Exodus 2 text). The most significant LXX divergences from the MT in this chapter are:

- **v.2:** LXX adds that the bush was "not consumed" (ou katekaeto), matching the MT's eynenu ukkal.
- **v.4:** Both MT and LXX double the name "Mosheh, Mosheh." Vaticanus reads Mouses Mouses clearly.
- **v.12:** LXX renders ehyeh as esomai ("I will be"), choosing the future tense where the Hebrew allows both present and future.
- **v.14:** The most theologically significant divergence: MT's Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh becomes ego eimi ho on ("I am the Being One/the One Who Is"). The LXX shifts from the verbal, dynamic Hebrew self-predication to an ontological, participial Greek construction. This reading profoundly influenced Christian theology and Greek philosophy's engagement with the biblical God.
- **v.15:** LXX reads kurios for YHWH throughout, as is standard Septuagintal practice. The divine name is never transliterated in Vaticanus.
- **v.19:** LXX clarifies the difficult ve-lo' be-yad hazaqah as ean me meta cheiros krataias ("unless with a mighty hand"), resolving an ambiguity the MT preserves.
- **v.22:** Both MT and LXX include the despoiling motif. LXX uses skuleusite, a military term for stripping spoil from enemies.

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 3 is well-preserved with no significant lacunae on pages 43-44. Column spacing is regular, and the uncial hand is clear and consistent.

-- EMET
